title: SGLang NPU launch requires --disable-cuda-graph and --skip-server-warmup flags
problem_description: SGLang's default launch behavior attempts CUDA graph capture and server warmup, both of which fail on Ascend NPU: (1) CUDA graph capture is a CUDA-specific feature not available on NPU, causing the server to crash during startup; (2) server warmup exercises operator patterns that may not have NPU implementations, causing spurious failures. Without these flags, the SGLang server fails to start or returns errors during initial requests.
rough_fix_approach: When launching SGLang on NPU (backend=ascend), always append --disable-cuda-graph and --skip-server-warmup to the sglang serve command. Additionally, --trust-remote-code should be added when the model uses custom HuggingFace model code (common for OCR/VLM models). These flags can be auto-injected by the serving validation script during Phase 3/Phase 5.
artifact_evidence: validated/phase_3_entry_script_canonical.json:lines 141-150 (npu_runtime_checks include 'cuda_nccl_markers_absent', 'no_cpu_fallback' — evidence these flags prevent fallback)

involved_code_files.role: Serving validation script — constructs the sglang launch command with NPU-appropriate flags
SGLang CLI entry point — the serve subcommand accepts --disable-cuda-graph and --skip-server-warmup

reasoning: Every SGLang-based NPU migration will encounter this. CUDA graph capture is fundamentally CUDA-specific; it will NEVER work on Ascend NPU. Server warmup calls operator paths that may not have NPU implementations, and skipping it avoids false negatives. The flags are deterministic — either they're present and the server launches cleanly, or they're absent and the server crashes during graph capture. This can be enforced as a default in the Phase 3 entry script generation for sglang_serving routes. Confidence is 0.90 because while the pattern is clear, future CANN versions or SGLang versions may handle these differently (e.g., SGLang could auto-detect NPU and skip CUDA graphs internally).
